segments.s118-g17: سزای تو دیدی is literally 'your recompense you saw' (object + possessor + past verb); دیدی is 2nd-person past ('you saw'). The following که introduces a content clause (indirect question/exclamation): که یزدان چه کرد = 'what God did' (یزدان as subject, چه as interrogative/exclamative 'what/how', کرد past tense).
segments.s118-g18: ز دیو و ز جادو is a poetic use of the preposition ز (from/by/because of) repeated for emphasis: 'from the demon and from the sorcery.' برآورد گرد is a compound/resultative verbal phrase: برآورد (brought/removed, perfective) with گرد (from گردیدن) producing a resultative/passive sense 'was driven out/expelled' — so the phrase means 'he expelled/drove out them.' Word order places the source phrases before the verb complex.
segments.s118-g19: کنون‌گر functions as an archaic conditional 'کنون گر' = 'if now'; شوی is the 2nd-person present-subjunctive/optative 'you become' (شوی = 'should become' / 'become'); آگه takes the preposition از to mark source/topic, so آگه از روزگار = 'aware of the course/ways of time (or fate)'.
segments.s118-g20: روان و خرد are the two nouns 'soul/spirit and wisdom' placed before the optative predicate. بادت is the optative construction (باد = 'may it be' with enclitic -ت marking 'to you'): 'may ... be to you'; آموزگار is the predicate noun 'teacher.' Together: 'May soul and wisdom be your teacher(s).' The clause is optative rather than an indicative statement.
segments.s118-g21: همانجا بمان is an imperative request: همانجا (adverb) 'there' followed by بمان (imperative of ماندن) 'remain/stay'. تاج مازندران is a nominative/title phrase placed after the imperative as a vocative: 'O crown of Mazandaran' (title + proper name). Note the poetic juxtaposition (no ezāfe linking) that makes تاج modify مازندران in the possessive/title sense.
segments.s118-g22: بدین بارگاه آی is another imperative clause: بدین = 'to this' (classical prepositional compound equivalent to 'to/into this'), بارگاه 'palace/court' is the goal, and آی is the archaic/poetic imperative 'come!'. چون that follows as a comparative particle 'like/as', and کهتران (plural of کهتر 'inferior/younger') completes the comparison 'like the lesser ones'. Word order is typical poetic Persian where the prepositional element and imperative precede the comparative phrase.
segments.s118-g23: که here functions as a causal conjunction 'since/because'. با is the preposition 'with/by'; چنگ رستم is a genitive/possessive phrase 'Rostam's harp' (poetry often omits an explicit ezāfe). ندارید is present second-person plural negative 'you do not have/possess'. تاو is the direct object placed after the verb (poetic word order) and here means 'radiance/glow'.
segments.s118-g24: بده is the imperative 'give!' (addressing the addressee). زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the verb. بر کام ما is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto our desire' (بر + noun + possessor pronoun ما), meaning 'to satisfy our wish'. باژ و ساو are two coordinated nouns; in this line they function as the items/words to be given or as names/terms associated with the demand — the exact identity is lexical/onomatopoeic, but grammatically they are a noun phrase joined by و.
segments.s118-g1: یکی نامه‌ای is a noun phrase meaning 'a certain letter' (یکی = 'a certain' placed before the noun; نامه‌ای is the indefinite/partitive form of نامه). بر + noun is a prepositional phrase 'on/over' and here بر حریر سپید = 'on white silk' with the adjective سپید following the noun حریر in this poetic order.
segments.s118-g2: بدو is a dative pronominal adverb 'to it / unto it' (pointing back to the letter). اندرون is a locative 'inside', so بدو اندرون = 'into/inside it' (archaic word order). چند is a quantifier 'some/a few' modifying the following nouns بیم و امید 'fears and hopes'.
segments.s118-g3: دبیری خردمند is a noun phrase meaning 'the (office/skill of a) wise secretary' used here to indicate a wise scribe; the adjective خردمند follows the noun. بنوشت is an archaic past verbal form 'wrote' (the provided lemma/senses did not include the verb sense), and خوب is an adverb 'well' modifying the verb.
segments.s118-g4: پدید آورید is a compound verbal idea 'made manifest / brought forth' (پدید = 'manifest/appear', آورید = past 'brought/caused'). اندرو = 'in it/therein' (an enclitic locative/pronominal form). زشت و خوب are the coordinated nouns 'the ugly and the good' that are the objects of آوردن.
segments.s118-g5: نخست is an adverb 'first/at first'. آفرین here functions as 'praise / acclamation' and with کرد ('did/made') forms 'praised' (آفرین کردن = to praise). بر introduces the person praised (indirect object): بر دادگر = 'the just one' (the ruler/judge).
segments.s118-g6: کزو is a relative/adverbial 'from whom / from which' linking back to the praised one. دید پیدا is a copular/periphrastic expression 'was seen / became manifest' (دید = past of 'see/be seen', پیدا = 'apparent/manifest'). به گیتی هنر = 'skill/art in the world' with به marking location ('in the world'). The whole clause modifies or explains the praised one's renown.
segments.s118-g7: This line contains two coordinated clauses. خرد داد = 'he gave wisdom' (خرد = 'wisdom', داد = past 'gave' here). و joins the clauses, and گردان سپهر آفرید = 'he created the revolving/turning sky' (گردان as an adjectival participle 'revolving' modifying سپهر; آفرید = 'created'). Note the poetic order and simple past verbs.
segments.s118-g8: A coordinated list of abstract nouns (درشتی و تندی و مهر) followed by the verb آفرید, so 'he created roughness and fierceness and love.' Each noun is connected by و and the final آفرید applies to all three items.
segments.s118-g25: وگر is an archaic conditional 'if' (equivalent to modern اگر). گاه مازندران is a temporal genitive phrase 'the time/occasion of Mazandaran' (noun + noun), and بایدت is the modal predicate باید with the 2nd‑person enclitic -ت, meaning 'it is necessary for you' / 'you must'. Word order is verb‑final/after the time phrase, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s118-g26: مگر here means 'except / perhaps / unless' introducing the exception/possibility. زین is the noun 'saddle / harness' used with نشان 'mark, sign' (زین نشان = 'this saddle's mark' or 'this token of the saddle'). راه is the object 'road/way', and بگشایدت is a 3rd‑person form with enclitic -ت: an optative/causative sense 'may it open for you' / 'it opens for you'. The clause expresses a possible means by which the road will be opened to you.
segments.s118-g27: وگرنه = 'otherwise'. چو here functions as a temporal/subordinate conjunction 'when' (classical usage). ارژنگ و دیو سپید are coordinated subjects/agents ('Arzhang and the White Demon'), with سپید post‑modifying دیو ('the white demon').
segments.s118-g28: دلت is دل + 2nd‑person enclitic -ت 'your heart'. کرد is the past tense of 'to do/make' used causatively here ('made / will make'). باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary', and the predicate ز جان ناامید combines ز (poetic 'of/from') + جان ('life') + ناامید ('hopeless') to mean 'to despair of life'. Literally: 'they will make your heart must/be compelled to be hopeless of life' → 'they will make your heart despair of life.'
segments.s118-g29: بخواند is a 3rd‑person verbal form (future/optative/poetic) meaning 'he will call/summon'. آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time'. شاه فرهاد را is the object phrase 'Farhad, the king' or 'the king Farhad' marked by the object/definite marker را; here فرهاد is the person being summoned and شاه is his title.
segments.s118-g30: گراینده is a present‑participle/agent noun 'one who directs/turns' used as an epithet meaning 'wielder/brandisher'. تیغ پولاد is a genitive compound 'sword of steel' (تیغ 'sword' + پولاد 'steel'), and the final را repeats the object marker in apposition: 'Farhad, the wielder of the steel sword.'
segments.s118-g31: گزین here functions as a nominal/epithet 'the chosen (one)'. بزرگان آن شهر is a genitive phrase 'the great men of that city' (بزرگان = elders/great ones, آن = that/the), and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause says 'he was the chosen of the city's great men.'
segments.s118-g32: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. بی‌کاری 'idleness' and رنج 'suffering / hardship' are coordinated causes; بی‌بهر is a predicate/adjective 'without benefit / useless', and the final بود is the past copula 'was'. Together: 'because of idleness and hardship he was of no use.'
segments.s118-g9: به نیک و به بد is a prepositional phrase meaning “for good and for ill / in good and in bad”; the two به are the same preposition repeated for balance. دادمان is a past (classical) verbal form meaning “gave to us” (داد + ما + ن (old enclitic)); دستگاه is the direct object meaning “system/arrangement.” Word order is prepositional phrase + verb + object, a common poetic sequence.
segments.s118-g10: خداوند is the head noun “God / lord.” گردنده is an agentive/participle-like epithet “the one who turns / who causes to revolve,” modifying or appositional to خداوند; خورشید و ماه are the objects of that action (the things turned). This is a compact attributive phrase: “God, the turner of sun and moon.”
segments.s118-g11: اگر introduces a conditional clause. دادگر باشی uses باشی (classical/subjunctive-present form) with دادگر to mean “if you are just.” پاک دین is an adjectival + noun collocation meaning “pure in religion / pure of faith.” The clause reads “If (you) are just and pure in faith.”
segments.s118-g12: ز هر کس = “from every/any person” (هر کس here = “anyone”). نیابی is the classical present/future-form of یاب (to find/get) with the negative prefix already attached (نی‑), so literally “you will not find”; combined with به جز (“except”) the sense becomes “you will find from no one except …” — i.e. “you will receive nothing but praise.” آفرین is “praise/acclamation.”
segments.s118-g13: وگر = “but if / however if.” بدنشان literally “their body/selves/persons” and here means “if you are of them / belong to them.” باشی is again the subjunctive/present form “are” used in the protasis. بدکنش is a substantive/adjective meaning “ill‑doer / ill‑behaved” (an evildoer). The clause says “But if you are of them and an evildoer…”
segments.s118-g14: ز چرخ بلند is a source phrase “from the lofty wheel/sky” (چرخ = heaven/sky/fate; بلند = lofty/high). آیدت is the verb “it comes to you” (آید + enclitic ت for “to you”); classical narrative uses this present/future sense for what will befall someone. سرزنش is the noun “reproach.” Literal order: “From the lofty heaven will come to you reproach.”
segments.s118-g15: جهاندار = “the sovereign / ruler.” اگر marks the conditional. دادگر is the adjective “just.” باشدی is an archaic/subjunctive verbal form (classical optative/subjunctive) used here with the ruler as subject, equivalent to modern باشد “be” in a conditional sense: “if the sovereign is just.”
segments.s118-g16: ز فرمان او = “from his command / decree.” کی here is a rhetorical interrogative (literally “who/when”) and functions like “how / who would” in English rhetorical questions. گذر باشدی uses گذر “passage/escape” with باشدی (archaic subjunctive) meaning “would there be any escape?” So the line asks rhetorically: if the ruler is just, who could escape his command?
segments.s118-g33: بدو here is a dative pronoun meaning 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb (common in older Persian). گفت is the narrative past 'said' (the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense, so its sense is marked below). کاین is the fused form of 'که این' meaning 'that/this' used to introduce the following noun phrase; نامه پندمند = 'an admonishing letter' where پندمند is an adjective meaning 'full of counsel/admonition'.
segments.s118-g34: ببر is the imperative 'take!' (object = the letter). سوی is the preposition 'toward/to', and آن دیو is 'that demon'. جسته is a participle/past adjective 'having leapt/sprung' placed after the noun (a common poetic order) and ز بند = 'from (his) bonds/fetters' (ز = 'from', بند = 'fetter, bond').
segments.s118-g35: چو + past verb (= چو ... بشنید) forms a temporal clause 'when ... heard'. از شاه بشنید = 'heard from the king'. فرهاد is the subject; in older/poetic syntax the subject can follow the verb. گرد is an adverbial here (roughly 'around' / linking to the following object phrase) and belongs with the action 'ببوسید زمین' in the next segment.
segments.s118-g36: زمین را is the definite object with the object marker را: 'the ground/earth'. ببوسید is the past tense 'he kissed'. و links the two actions; نامه ببرد = 'and he took/carried the letter' (ببرد here functions as the narrative past/carry meaning in this context).
segments.s118-g37: به شهری = 'in/at a city' (به + locative noun). کجا introduces a relative/locative sense 'where'. سست پایان is an adjectival phrase; poetically it describes a place 'where the end (پایان) is weak/vulnerable' or more generally 'where the weak come to their end'. بدند is an archaic/past plural copular/existential 'there were / they were'.
segments.s118-g38: This is a simple nominal sentence: سواران 'horsemen' apposed to پولادخایان 'the steel-clad ones/steel‑armoured warriors' (a compound epithet). بدند repeats the past-plural 'there were/they were', linking the existence of these riders to the place just described.
segments.s118-g39: هم is 'also' / 'and'. آنکس که بودند = 'those people who were' (relative clause introduced by که). پا از دوال is an idiomatic/archaic phrase: پا = 'step/foot', از = 'from', and دوال is an archaic or place-name term here; together it conveys 'who had set foot in / who came from Dūwāl' or a related provenance/qualification. The exact lexical meaning of دوال is irregular/archaic in classical Persian.
segments.s118-g40: لقبشان = 'their epithet/title' (لقب + possessive suffix). چنین بود = 'was thus/such'. بسیار سال here is an adverbial time expression 'for many years' (بسیار = 'many', سال = 'years'), placed after the predicate as is common in the poem.
segments.s118-g41: بدان is the prepositional phrase 'be + ān' (here 'in/at that'), followed by شهر 'city'. بد modifies شاه (not شهر): the phrase reads 'the city of the bad/evil king (شاه) of Mazandaran' with the possessive/name مازندران placed after شاه. The order is poetic: preposition + demonstrative, then the location noun, then the adjectival epithet + head noun + place name.
segments.s118-g42: هم آنجا is an emphatic/location phrase 'also there' (هم = 'also', آنجا = 'there'). دلیران و کندآوران is a nominative plural listing 'the brave ones and the kand-āvarān' with no finite verb expressed (elliptical 'were' is understood in poetry). The conjunction و simply links the two groups.
segments.s118-g43: چو + بشنید = 'when he heard' (چو here = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'). کز is the fused conjunction/relative 'that'/'which' introducing the reported content; نزد means 'in the presence of' or 'from the court of' and links to کاووس شاه 'King Kavus' (name + title).
segments.s118-g44: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite past/agentive form 'one sent' = 'a messenger' (literally 'a sent one'); باهش = با + اش 'to/with him' (i.e. 'to him'); آمد is the past tense 'came'; ز راه = 'from the road' or 'along the way' (ز = 'from', راه = 'road/way'). The phrase presents the arrival of that messenger to the addressee.
segments.s118-g45: پذیره شدن is a nominalized verbal phrase 'the being-received' / 'the reception'; را is the definite/object marker making it a specific event; سپاه گران = 'the heavy/weighty army' (گران used as an intensifier 'mighty/important host'). Word order places the reception phrase before the agent (the host) for emphasis.
segments.s118-g46: دلیران و شیران مازندران lists the agents: 'the brave ones and the شیران (literally 'lions', here a metaphor for brave men) of Mazandaran.' The place-name مازندران post-modifies both groups. The noun شیران functions metaphorically as 'heroes/lions' in a martial sense.
segments.s118-g47: ز لشکر = 'from the army/host' (ز = 'from'); یکایک همه is a distributive/emphatic phrase 'one by one, every one' (یکایک = 'one by one', همه = 'all/everyone'); برگزید is the past 'he chose/selected.' The word order places the source first, then the distributive, then the verb.
segments.s118-g48: ازیشان = از + ایشان 'from them'; هنر خواست = 'he asked/wanted skill' (هنر = 'skill/excellence', خواست = past 'he desired/asked for'). کاید پدید is a purposive/result clause: کاید ('cunning/plot') and پدید ('appear' — here presenting the hoped-for manifestation). Together they mean he demanded skill of them that cunning/ability might be revealed.
segments.s118-g65: به دل گفت is literally 'to the heart he said' — the indirect object (به دل) precedes the verb گفت ('he said'), a common Persian order meaning 'he told himself' or 'he said to his heart.' پنهان شود آفتاب is a subordinate jussive/subjunctive clause: پنهان (an adjective/participle 'hidden') + شود (subjunctive/passive 'be'/'become') forms 'let it be hidden' or 'be hidden,' and آفتاب (the noun) appears after the verb phrase by poetic inversion ('let the sun be hidden').
segments.s118-g66: شب آید is a short clause 'night will come' with آید expressing present/future action. بود گاه آرام و خواب is a second clause predicated with بود ('was') meaning 'it was a time of calm and sleep' — گاه (time) modified by the adjectives آرام (calm) and خواب (sleep); note the mixed tenses (آید present/future vs. بود past) is a poetic narrative choice linking expectation ('night will come') to the resulting/characteristic state ('it was a time of calm and sleep').
segments.s118-g67: ز رستم is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of Rostam.' The construction نخواهد ... آرمید uses نخواهد (a future/negative auxiliary) before the past-stem آرمید to express future negative: 'will not rest.' جهان is the logical subject ('the world'), placed between the auxiliary and the verb in this poetic order: 'the world will not rest.'
segments.s118-g68: نخواهد شدن is a future-negative modal construction ('will not become'); شدن is the infinitive/masdar 'to become.' نام او ناپدید is the noun phrase 'his name [be] vanished' where ناپدید is an adjective/predicative complement. Put together: 'his name will not become vanished' → 'his name will not disappear.' The clause order is inverted for poetic effect (modal + شدن before the noun phrase).
segments.s118-g69: غمی گشت is 'sorrow became/rose' — گشت (past of گشتن) here means 'came to be' or 'arose.' از ارژنگ و دیو سپید is the source/cause 'from Arzhang and the white demon,' with the adjective سپید following the noun دیو (standard noun–adjective order).
segments.s118-g70: که شد introduces an explanatory/casual clause 'for/that it happened that...' و شد (شد used as a light verb meaning 'happened') combines with the participle کشته to form a passive/result: 'it happened that [they] were killed.' The sequence کشته پولاد غندی و بید lists the victims (pocketing the participle before the names by poetic word order): 'Polād, Ghandī and Bīd were killed.'
segments.s118-g71: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when.' آن نامه شاه یکسر بخواند is 'the king read that letter in one go/throughout' — یکسر ('completely/at once') modifies the verb بخواند ('read'), and the verb at the end marks the action of the king in that temporal clause ('when the king read the letter through').
segments.s118-g72: دو دیده is 'two eyes' (object). به خون دل اندر is a complex locative phrase 'into the blood of the heart' (به + خون + دل with اندر 'within' reinforcing the locative). نشاند is an archaic/transitive past verb meaning 'placed/settled' (here figuratively 'sank/settled his eyes'); the verb comes at the end in classical word order, so the whole means 'he set/sank his two eyes into the blood of his heart.'
segments.s118-g57: نگشت is the past-negative (did not become/turn) of گشتن placed first for emphasis; ایچ is an emphatic negator meaning “not at all.” فرهاد را uses the object/definite marker را after the proper name to mark whose face is being described. روی (noun) is followed by the adjective زرد (pale/wan) — adjective after noun is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s118-g58: نیامد is a past tense negative (‘did not come/arrive’); here it functions impersonally with sufferings as subject (i.e. sufferings did not befall him). برو is an archaic/poetic form likely functioning as a clitic/prepositional element ('بر او' = 'upon him') and is not the ordinary imperative sense, so its exact modern gloss is irregular. رنج بسیار و درد is a coordinated noun phrase: رنج modified by بسیار (‘much’/‘great’) and linked by و to درد (‘pain’).
segments.s118-g59: ببردند is a past transitive verb in the 3rd-person plural (‘they took/brought’). فرهاد را again carries the object marker را. نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘into the presence of / before the king,’ with نزد following the object as is normal in narrative word order.
segments.s118-g60: ز (short for از) introduces the source/about-whom something is asked; here پرسید is a past 3rd-person singular verb (‘asked’). The conjunction و links the following phrase ز رنج راه, where ز again means ‘about’/‘concerning’ (or ‘from’ in the sense of ‘on account of’), and رنج راه is a genitive-like phrase ‘the hardship of the road’ (رنج modified by راه).
segments.s118-g61: پس is a linking adverb meaning ‘then’ (narrative sequence). آن نامه is the definite object ‘that letter’; بنهاد is the classical past form of نهادن (he placed/put) used here in a narrative register. پیش دبیر is a prepositional phrase ‘before/in the presence of the scribe/secretary,’ with پیش meaning ‘in front of / before’.
segments.s118-g62: می‌و مشک (classical poetic pairing) are items placed with the letter; انداخته is a past participle (lit. ‘having placed/laid’) describing how the packet was prepared. پر before حریر is the adjective-like use ‘full of / filled with’ so پر حریر = ‘wrapped with / full of silk’ (poetic phrasing). Note that می‌و here is an archaic/poetic form and does not align exactly with modern senses.
segments.s118-g63: چو آگه شد is a temporal clause: چو = ‘when’ and آگه شد = ‘became aware / learned.’ از marks the object/source of awareness (‘of/about Rostam’), so از رستم = ‘of Rostam.’ The conjunction و links کار دیو (‘the deed/act of the demon’) as the other thing he learned about; note the noun کار precedes its modifier دیو (genitival relationship shown by juxtaposition).
segments.s118-g64: پر از خون شدش is a causative/result clause: پر از خون = ‘full of blood,’ شدش is شد + enclitic ش (meaning ‘it became to him’ / ‘befell him’), so ‘his [eyes] were filled with blood.’ دیده دل lists two body-nouns (‘eye, heart’) in a compact apposition; پرغریو (an adjective/compound) means ‘full of cries/clamor.’ Grammatically, شدش governs the state expressed by the following noun phrase.
segments.s118-g49: چنین is an adverb meaning "thus/so," placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the past tense of گفتن "said" (here used in the simple past; note the supplied senses list did not include the verbal sense, so that sense is flagged separately). کامروز is a temporal adverb "that day/that very day" placed between verb and subject (a common poetic inversion). فرزانگی is a nominative (the personified noun "wisdom") appearing after the verb — Persian poetry often uses verb–subject order.
segments.s118-g50: جدا کرد نتوان is a two-part negated ability: جدا کردن "to separate/detach" + کرد (make/do) + نتوان "could not/was unable to." The literal structure is "could not make (it) separate," i.e. "could not be separated" or "could not be distinguished." ز is the poetic preposition "from," and دیوانگی is the noun "madness/frenzy." The whole clause states inability to separate (something) from madness.
segments.s118-g51: همه modifies the following compound "راه و رسم" (= "ways and manners/customs") meaning "all/every." پلنگ is an attributive noun placed after the phrase (typical Persian order: noun phrase + modifier) meaning "of the leopard" (literal or figurative). آورید is the 2nd-person plural imperative (or hortative) "bring"/"produce." The line is a command: "Bring every leopard-way and manner."
segments.s118-g52: سر is the direct object "head(s)," هوشمندان is a plural nominal form meaning "the wise ones" (literally "intelligent [ones]") used here as the possessed/affected group. به introduces the instrument/goal "into/into the grasp of," چنگ is "claw" (not the musical sense here), and آورید again is the imperative "bring/seize." The idiom به چنگ آوردن means "to seize into the claw" = "to capture/seize."
segments.s118-g53: پذیره ("reception/acceptance") plus شدندش is an idiomatic construction: شدند (they became/did) with the enclitic -ش (= him) yields "they received him" or "they made him welcome." پر از چین is a stative phrase "full of wrinkles" (پر = "full (of)", از marks the thing full of, چین = "wrinkle"). به روی places the location "on the face" (به + روی).
segments.s118-g54: سخنشان is سخن ("speech/words") with the possessive/associative suffix شان (here written as part of the token) = "their/their words." نرفت is the simple past "did not go," used idiomatically to mean "had no effect/took no hold." ایچ is an emphatic negative "not at all/nothing." بر here means "toward/on" and آرزوی is "wish/desire." Together: "Their words did not at all go toward his desire" = "their words did not move his wish in the least."
segments.s118-g55: یکی is an indefinite pronoun "one/someone/a certain (person)." دست بگرفت is the compound past tense "took/grasped the hand" (بگرفت = seized). و coordinates the clauses. بفشاردش is a past/actional form of فشاریدن with the enclitic object -ش ("him/it") meaning "and squeezed him/it." In classical poetry these prefixes (بـ) and enclitics attach to form compact verbs.
segments.s118-g56: پی و استخوانها is a customary pair meaning "flesh (or sinew/tendons) and bones." In older/poetic Persian پی commonly denotes fleshy parts; استخوانها is "the bones." بیازاردش (bi-āzārad + -ش) is a causative/active form meaning "he tormented/afflicted him" or "it pained him." The clause says the act afflicted his flesh and bones.

sentences.s118-s9: You have seen your due — what God did in driving out the demon and the sorcery.
sentences.s118-s10: If now you become aware of the ways of time, let soul and wisdom be your teacher(s).
sentences.s118-s11: Stay there, O crown of Mazandaran; come to this palace like the lesser ones.
sentences.s118-s12: Since you do not possess the radiance of Rostam's harp, give it quickly to satisfy our desire — Bázh and Sáv.
sentences.s118-s1: A certain letter on white silk — inside it, some fears and hopes.
sentences.s118-s2: A wise secretary (or scribe) wrote it well, and therein brought forth the ugly and the good.
sentences.s118-s3: First he praised the just one; from him skill became manifest in the world.
sentences.s118-s4: He gave wisdom and made the revolving heavens; he created roughness, fierceness, and love.
sentences.s118-s13: And if the time of Mazandaran requires you, perhaps this saddle‑mark will open the road for you.
sentences.s118-s14: Otherwise, when Arzhang and the White Demon (come), your heart will be made to despair of life.
sentences.s118-s15: At that time the king will summon Farhad, the wielder of the steel sword.
sentences.s118-s16: He was the chosen one of the great men of that city; because of idleness and suffering he was of no benefit.
sentences.s118-s5: God, the one who turns the sun and the moon, gave us a system for good and for ill.
sentences.s118-s6: If you are just and pure in religion, you will receive from every person nothing but praise.
sentences.s118-s7: But if you belong to them and are an evildoer, reproach will come to you from the lofty sky.
sentences.s118-s8: If the sovereign is just, how could anyone escape his command?
sentences.s118-s17: He said to him, "Take this admonishing letter to that demon who has sprung from his bonds."
sentences.s118-s18: When Farhad heard it from the king, he kissed the ground and took the letter.
sentences.s118-s19: In a city where the weak meet their end, there stood horsemen — steel-clad warriors.
sentences.s118-s20: And those men who had set foot in Dūwāl — such was their epithet for many years.
sentences.s118-s21: In that city of the evil king of Mazandaran there, too, were brave men and kand-āvarān.
sentences.s118-s22: When he heard that (someone) had been sent from the presence of King Kavus, a messenger came to him from the road.
sentences.s118-s23: The reception was by the mighty host — the brave men and the lions of Mazandaran.
sentences.s118-s24: From the host he selected each one, every man; he demanded skill from them so that cunning might appear.
sentences.s118-s33: He said to his heart, "Let the sun be hidden; night will come — it was a time of calm and sleep."
sentences.s118-s34: Because of Rostam the world will not rest; his name will not be made to vanish.
sentences.s118-s35: Sorrow arose from Arzhang and the white demon, for Polād, Ghandī and Bīd were slain.
sentences.s118-s36: When the king read that letter straight through, he sank his two eyes into the blood of his heart.
sentences.s118-s29: Farhad's face did not turn pale at all; great suffering and pain did not come upon him.
sentences.s118-s30: They took Farhad before the king; (the king) asked Kavus and about the hardships of the road.
sentences.s118-s31: Then he placed that letter before the secretary, having put wine and musk, wrapped in silk.
sentences.s118-s32: When he learned of Rostam and the demon's deed, his eyes filled with blood and his heart full of cries.
sentences.s118-s25: On that day wisdom spoke thus: it could not be separated from madness.
sentences.s118-s26: Bring all the leopard's ways and manners; seize the heads of the wise in your claw.
sentences.s118-s27: They received him; his face was full of wrinkles; their words did not in the least move toward his wish.
sentences.s118-s28: Someone seized his hand and squeezed it, and troubled his flesh and bones.
